title: A Denser Planar Surface Code
authors: Guang Hao Low, William J. Huggins, Dominic W. Berry, Tanuj Khattar, Alec F. White, Nicholas C. Rubin, Ryan Babbush
abstract: We present a quantum code implementable on a regular $2$D hex grid with an estimated encoding rate up to $4.5\times$ of that of a rotated surface code patch using circuit-level noise in a one- and two-qubit $10^{-3}$ error uniform depolarizing model. Our approach is based on yoking a dense packing of surface code twist defects, enabled by new stabilizer measurement cycles with an optimal four layers of nearest-neighbor two-qubit gates, almost no distance-reducing hook errors, and efficient decoding. We demonstrate a space-efficient architecture for computing on densely packed logical qubits, including new padding-free lattice surgery protocols in an optimal bounding box of $2d^2$ data and measurement qubits per patch. Assuming a $1μ$s surface code cycle time and a $10μ$s reaction time, these developments enable chemically accurate ground state phase estimation of a broad class of `utility-scale' electronic structure simulation problems such as the $108$ spin-orbital FeMoco-based nitrogen fixation catalyst in under a month with $89$k noisy superconducting qubits. We elucidate a Pareto frontier of space-time trade-offs and find a minimum physical quantum volume of $1.3$ mega-qubit-hours. These correspond to a $36\times$ space and $6.6\times$ spacetime improvement, respectively, over our previous state-of-the-art minimum-Toffoli resource estimates (Phys. Rev. X 15, 041016).
summary: This paper introduces a novel, denser planar surface code architecture by efficiently packing twist defects into a 2D hex grid. This advancement boosts the encoding rate by 4.5x, leading to substantial reductions in the required physical qubits and computational time for simulating complex electronic structures, such as nitrogen fixation catalysts.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving utility-scale quantum simulation for electronic structure problems while drastically reducing the required physical quantum resources.
main_result: The authors propose a dense packing of surface code twist defects, achieving an estimated encoding rate up to 4.5x that of standard surface codes, leading to significant improvements in resource estimates.
method: The work develops new stabilizer measurement cycles and padding-free lattice surgery protocols to implement a denser, more efficient planar surface code architecture.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum computer utilizing the surface code for fault-tolerant quantum error correction, implemented on a regular 2D hex grid. The target application is ground state phase estimation for electronic structure problems.
key_observables: Encoding rate (up to 4.5x improvement), minimum physical quantum volume (1.3 mega-qubit-hours), and resource trade-offs across space, time, and qubit count.
important_parameters: Noise model: one- and two-qubit $10^{-3}$ error uniform depolarizing model. Time scales: $1\mu$s cycle time, $10\mu$s reaction time. Target: Chemical accuracy for FeMoco catalyst.
main_assumptions: Assumes an ideal outer decoder for lattice surgery and uses circuit-level noise modeling; the comparison to prior art relies on specific resource estimates (e.g., Toffoli gate counts).
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the Pareto frontier of physical resources for ground state phase estimation, comparing the new dense-packing approach against prior art across 'Compact', 'Balanced', 'Efficient', and 'Fast' regimes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first identifying the resource bottleneck of standard surface codes, then introducing the dense-packing twist defect strategy, detailing the optimized lattice surgery protocols, and finally applying the resulting resource estimates to benchmark utility-scale quantum simulation tasks.
why_interesting: The focus on optimizing resource scaling for quantum simulation, particularly by improving the encoding rate of topological codes, directly impacts the feasibility timeline for quantum chemistry simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Pareto frontier of physical resources for chemically-accurate ground state phase estimation of ‘utility-scale’ benchmark molecules with 54 to 150 orbitals, assuming quantum hardware with nearest-neighbor connectivity, a 10−3 physical error rate, a surface code cycle time of 1µs, and a reaction time of 10µs. Shown are estimates for the FeMo-cofactor for nitrogen fixation, the XVIII configuration of a Ruthenium-based catalyst for carbon fixation, and the Cpd1X configuration of a metabolic cofactor p450. We present new space-time tradeoffs in quantum circuits and quantum error correction for compiling the second-quantized Double-Factorized-Tensor-HyperContracted (DFTHC) electronic...

FIG. 2. Summary of our key contributions. Section II: We reduce the lattice surgery bounding box of a distance d rotated surface code patch from 2(d + 1)2 to 2d2 physical qubits. Section III: We introduce an optimal-depth, hook-error-free, and efficiently decodable implementation of surface code twist defects using nearest-neighbor gates on a degree-3 hex grid. This enables a packing of three logical qubits into a rectangular patch with a 1.5× encoding rate. By merging boundaries of three-qubit rectangles, we obtain a dense packing of logical qubits in twist defects with asymptotically a 2× encoding rate. By yoking this dense packing of twist defects, we obtain logical qubit “cold storage”...

In this work, we demonstrate (right) a new Z-top configuration that permits lattice surgery operations within a bounding box of 2d2 qubits per patch.
